'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' are two {{ill|Kami|ja|神道の神|lt=Shinto deities}} appearing in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythological]] [[deity (Shinto)|female deities]]. In one book of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}} (father) and {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}} (brother). The sister goddess Ōyatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|大屋都比売神|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|大屋津姫命|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, and the sister goddess Tsumatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|抓津姫神|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|抓津姫命|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami|ja|都麻都比売命|lt=Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami|ja|爪津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫命|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, among others. Together with {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, under the command of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}}, they scattered tree species across all the mountains nationwide and returned to {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|lt=Kii Province}} (now {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}). == Overview == [[File:Moriya Family Tree - 守矢氏系図.png|thumb|170px|{{Center|The first five generations of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢氏]] in 『守矢氏系譜』 (from Mote Yago to Hachikushi Kami)}} In the folk traditions of the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|lt=Suwa region}} ([[ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}), Ōyatsuhime is considered the offspring deity of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}, or an alias of that deity. She is also equated with {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s offspring deity {{ill|Uchi Prefecture Kami|ja|内県神|lt=Uchi Prefecture Kami}}. According to the 『Shuya Clan Genealogy』 (『守矢氏系譜』), established in the early Meiji period, Ōyatsuhime is the child of {{ill|Mote Takami|ja|洩宅神|lt=Mote Takami}} (son of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}) and is counted as the third generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}} who inherited ceremonial duties. Her name is said to originate from when her father hunted deer and captured 1,000 deer.
Mote Takami was born with extraordinary spiritual powers, took over his father's duties, carried bow and arrows, followed the Great Deity in hunting and game, and obtained a thousand deer. He had one son and named him Chikatō Kami. Chikatō Kami succeeded in overseeing ceremonial affairs. (Excerpt) Ancient [[Kagura|kagura]] songs: :Chikatō no Kitano Hayashinowa Susumushiwa Susumushiwa Yachiyo no Koyete Tsunenitaisenu :Chikatō no Myōjin Shiyushūreshito Ohosoransu Ohosoransu Yukitataima no Hana no Kiyomeyo Chikatō's shrines are located in the Suwa District's Nakatsusa: Aruga, Uehara, Hanahara, Yokobuki, Kyudo in the Higashichikuma District's Kanda and Hayashi. Situated in {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}}, there exists a shrine dedicated to him. In ancient times, he was the deity of over thirty villages within the district. His consort is {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}}. According to oral tradition, there is a shrine of him in the same area. {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}}, the offspring of the Great Deity's child {{ill|Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto|ja|片倉辺命|lt=Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto}}, is considered the fourth generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}}. He married {{ill|Mitasama Hime-no-Kami|ja|美都多麻比売神|lt=Mitasama Hime-no-Kami}}, the child of {{ill|Moritatsu-no-Kami|ja|守達神|lt=Moritatsu-no-Kami}}, and gave birth to {{ill|Hachikushi|ja|八櫛神|lt=Hachikushi}}.Suwa Education Association. "『守矢氏系譜』" 『Suwa Historical Documents Collection, Volume 28』, 1938, pp. 31-72.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp. 333-336.Imai Nohiku. "O Suwa-sama Faith and Kami no Naga" 『Gods' Village: Ancient Suwa Stories』, Kokusho Publishing, 1976, pp. 117-118.
According to the 『Genealogy』, Chikatō Kami married {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}} and moved to {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}} (now {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}}, {{ill|Kanda, Matsumoto|ja|神田|lt=Kanda}}), which suggests that Chikatō Kami was either exiled from Suwa or left voluntarily. It is also believed that the succession of Chikatō Kami by {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s grandchild {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}} indicates that the {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|lt=Suwa Clan}} (descendants of the mythological {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}) took over the local worship duties of indigenous forces. There are also traditions where the foreign-born Chikatō Kami married local daughters not only in Matsumoto but also in other places. A famous tale narrates that "Chikato Kami" from Shinshu came to {{ill|Akagiyama Shrine|ja|赤城神社|lt=Akagiyama Shrine}} and married the sister of the {{ill|Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神|lt=Akagi Shrine}}.Imai Nohiku. "Chikato-sama" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.325.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.342.. == Faith == According to Imai Nohiku's research on the distribution of {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}}, Chikatō Kami shrines are found not only in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} (13 shrines) but also in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (8 shrines), {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} (20 shrines), {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}} (7 shrines), and {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} (15 shrines), spreading from Kanto to Tohoku. They have various representations such as "Chikatō," "Chikata," "Chikato," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," and "Chikata," with pronunciations including "Chikata," "Chikatsu," "Chikato (u)," etc.Imai Nohiku. "Chikatō Kami Approach" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.326.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.343.. The distribution of Chikatō Kami shrines is characterized by their apparent movement along mountainous regions (such as the [[Yatsugatake|八ヶ岳]], [[Haruna Mountain|榛名山]], [[Akagi Mountain|赤城山]], [[Mount Ontake|男体山]], [[Yamigomezaki|八溝山]]) and is believed to have spread through the eastward expansion of hunting clans (descendants of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢族]], the so-called [[Mote Yago|洩矢族]]). As deities of hunting, they are closely related to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}. According to Imai's research, in Yamanashi, Saitama, and Gunma, {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} shrines coincide with Chikatō Kami shrines.Yamato Iwao. "Shinano Ancient History Study" 『Shinano Ancient History Study』, Meicho Publishing, 1990, pp.201-202.Imai Nohiku. "Survey Collection of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.212-281.. In addition to hunting deities from Suwa (Shinshu), there are regions where Chikatō Kami is also revered as a deity of agriculture and fire prevention, similar to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}, with many shrines worshiping them as {{ill|Ishibō|ja|石棒|lt=Ishibō}} deities.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp.347-349.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.300-301.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.338-339.Tanaka Moto. "Primitive Agricultural Ritual Elements in Mote Yashima Ceremonial System" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, p.192.. Moreover, many shrines have different main deities depending on the region, even within {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. There are still many unexplored shrine precincts and regions, leaving numerous mysteries unresolved. === Main Shrines === ==== Koshin Region ==== * {{ill|Kanda Chikatō Shrine|ja|神田千鹿頭神社|lt=Kanda revision.comment: Adding [[Category:ファイル]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' are two {{ill|Kami|ja|神道の神|lt=Shinto deities}} appearing in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythological]] [[deity (Shinto)|female deities]]. In one book of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}} (father) and {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}} (brother). The sister goddess Ōyatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|大屋都比売神|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|大屋津姫命|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, and the sister goddess Tsumatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|抓津姫神|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|抓津姫命|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami|ja|都麻都比売命|lt=Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami|ja|爪津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫命|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, among others. Together with {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, under the command of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}}, they scattered tree species across all the mountains nationwide and returned to {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|lt=Kii Province}} (now {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}). == Overview == [[File:Moriya Family Tree - 守矢氏系図.png|thumb|170px|{{Center|The first five generations of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢氏]] in 『守矢氏系譜』 (from Mote Yago to Hachikushi Kami)}} In the folk traditions of the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|lt=Suwa region}} ([[ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}), Ōyatsuhime is considered the offspring deity of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}, or an alias of that deity. She is also equated with {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s offspring deity {{ill|Uchi Prefecture Kami|ja|内県神|lt=Uchi Prefecture Kami}}. According to the 『Shuya Clan Genealogy』 (『守矢氏系譜』), established in the early Meiji period, Ōyatsuhime is the child of {{ill|Mote Takami|ja|洩宅神|lt=Mote Takami}} (son of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}) and is counted as the third generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}} who inherited ceremonial duties. Her name is said to originate from when her father hunted deer and captured 1,000 deer.
Mote Takami was born with extraordinary spiritual powers, took over his father's duties, carried bow and arrows, followed the Great Deity in hunting and game, and obtained a thousand deer. He had one son and named him Chikatō Kami. Chikatō Kami succeeded in overseeing ceremonial affairs. (Excerpt) Ancient [[Kagura|kagura]] songs: :Chikatō no Kitano Hayashinowa Susumushiwa Susumushiwa Yachiyo no Koyete Tsunenitaisenu :Chikatō no Myōjin Shiyushūreshito Ohosoransu Ohosoransu Yukitataima no Hana no Kiyomeyo Chikatō's shrines are located in the Suwa District's Nakatsusa: Aruga, Uehara, Hanahara, Yokobuki, Kyudo in the Higashichikuma District's Kanda and Hayashi. Situated in {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}}, there exists a shrine dedicated to him. In ancient times, he was the deity of over thirty villages within the district. His consort is {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}}. According to oral tradition, there is a shrine of him in the same area. {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}}, the offspring of the Great Deity's child {{ill|Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto|ja|片倉辺命|lt=Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto}}, is considered the fourth generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}}. He married {{ill|Mitasama Hime-no-Kami|ja|美都多麻比売神|lt=Mitasama Hime-no-Kami}}, the child of {{ill|Moritatsu-no-Kami|ja|守達神|lt=Moritatsu-no-Kami}}, and gave birth to {{ill|Hachikushi|ja|八櫛神|lt=Hachikushi}}.Suwa Education Association. "『守矢氏系譜』" 『Suwa Historical Documents Collection, Volume 28』, 1938, pp. 31-72.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp. 333-336.Imai Nohiku. "O Suwa-sama Faith and Kami no Naga" 『Gods' Village: Ancient Suwa Stories』, Kokusho Publishing, 1976, pp. 117-118.
According to the 『Genealogy』, Chikatō Kami married {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}} and moved to {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}} (now {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}}, {{ill|Kanda, Matsumoto|ja|神田|lt=Kanda}}), which suggests that Chikatō Kami was either exiled from Suwa or left voluntarily. It is also believed that the succession of Chikatō Kami by {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s grandchild {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}} indicates that the {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|lt=Suwa Clan}} (descendants of the mythological {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}) took over the local worship duties of indigenous forces. There are also traditions where the foreign-born Chikatō Kami married local daughters not only in Matsumoto but also in other places. A famous tale narrates that "Chikato Kami" from Shinshu came to {{ill|Akagiyama Shrine|ja|赤城神社|lt=Akagiyama Shrine}} and married the sister of the {{ill|Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神|lt=Akagi Shrine}}.Imai Nohiku. "Chikato-sama" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.325.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.342.. == Faith == According to Imai Nohiku's research on the distribution of {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}}, Chikatō Kami shrines are found not only in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} (13 shrines) but also in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (8 shrines), {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} (20 shrines), {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}} (7 shrines), and {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} (15 shrines), spreading from Kanto to Tohoku. They have various representations such as "Chikatō," "Chikata," "Chikato," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," and "Chikata," with pronunciations including "Chikata," "Chikatsu," "Chikato (u)," etc.Imai Nohiku. "Chikatō Kami Approach" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.326.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.343.. The distribution of Chikatō Kami shrines is characterized by their apparent movement along mountainous regions (such as the [[Yatsugatake|八ヶ岳]], [[Haruna Mountain|榛名山]], [[Akagi Mountain|赤城山]], [[Mount Ontake|男体山]], [[Yamigomezaki|八溝山]]) and is believed to have spread through the eastward expansion of hunting clans (descendants of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢族]], the so-called [[Mote Yago|洩矢族]]). As deities of hunting, they are closely related to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}. According to Imai's research, in Yamanashi, Saitama, and Gunma, {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} shrines coincide with Chikatō Kami shrines.Yamato Iwao. "Shinano Ancient History Study" 『Shinano Ancient History Study』, Meicho Publishing, 1990, pp.201-202.Imai Nohiku. "Survey Collection of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.212-281.. In addition to hunting deities from Suwa (Shinshu), there are regions where Chikatō Kami is also revered as a deity of agriculture and fire prevention, similar to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}, with many shrines worshiping them as {{ill|Ishibō|ja|石棒|lt=Ishibō}} deities.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp.347-349.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.300-301.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.338-339.Tanaka Moto. "Primitive Agricultural Ritual Elements in Mote Yashima Ceremonial System" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, p.192.. Moreover, many shrines have different main deities depending on the region, even within {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. There are still many unexplored shrine precincts and regions, leaving numerous mysteries unresolved. === Main Shrines === ==== Koshin Region ==== * {{ill|Kanda Chikatō Shrine|ja|神田千鹿頭神社|lt=Kanda Chikatō Shrine}}, {{ill|Hayashi Chikatō Shrine|ja|林千鹿頭神社|lt=Hayashi Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}} - Hataiwa) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Suwa City, Nagano|ja|諏訪市|lt=Suwa City}}, Toyoda Aruga) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|lt=Chino City}}, Chino Uehara) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|lt=Chino City}}, Yoneda Hanahara) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Yokobuki, Suwa City|ja|横吹|lt=Yokobuki}}, Suwa City) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Higashiina District, Ichigata Town|ja|上伊那郡|lt=Higashiina District, Ichigata Town}}, Higashiina District, Ichigata Town) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近津神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Kofu City, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kofu City}}, Kiyatecho) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Kofu City, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kofu City}}, Takamachi) ==== Kanto Region ==== * {{ill|Oogo Shrine|ja|大胡神社|lt=Oogo Shrine}} (Former {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}}) ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市|lt=Maebashi City}}, Kawaharaha) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kasukawa Town|ja|粕川町|lt=Kasukawa Town}}) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Battoi Town|ja|笂井町|lt=Battoi Town}}) * {{ill|Oyato Shrine|ja|親都神社|lt=Oyato Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District, Nakanojō Town|ja|吾妻郡|lt=Agatsuma District, Nakanojō Town}}) * {{ill|Chihō Shrine (Kazo Central)|ja|千方神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kazo City|ja|加須市|lt=Kazo City}}) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Kamijo)|ja|千勝社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Kamiji) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Honmachi)|ja|千勝神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Honmachi) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Yoshiba)|ja|千勝神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Yoshiba) * {{ill|Chikagata Shrine|ja|千形神社}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|熊谷市|lt=Kumagaya City}} - Honmachi) * {{ill|Chigata Shrine|ja|智賀都神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya City|ja|宇都宮市|lt=Utsunomiya City}} - Tokujircho) * {{ill|Chihō Shrine|ja|智方神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oyama City|ja|小山市|lt=Oyama City}} - Takei) * {{ill|Chiha Shrine|ja|血方神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi revision.comment: Adding [[Category:dardar]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' are two {{ill|Kami|ja|神道の神|lt=Shinto deities}} appearing in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythological]] [[deity (Shinto)|female deities]]. In one book of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}} (father) and {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}} (brother). The sister goddess Ōyatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|大屋都比売神|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|大屋津姫命|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, and the sister goddess Tsumatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|抓津姫神|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|抓津姫命|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami|ja|都麻都比売命|lt=Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami|ja|爪津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫命|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, among others. Together with {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, under the command of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}}, they scattered tree species across all the mountains nationwide and returned to {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|lt=Kii Province}} (now {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}). == Overview == [[File:Moriya Family Tree - 守矢氏系図.png|thumb|170px|{{Center|The first five generations of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢氏]] in 『守矢氏系譜』 (from Mote Yago to Hachikushi Kami)}} In the folk traditions of the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|lt=Suwa region}} ([[ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}), Ōyatsuhime is considered the offspring deity of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}, or an alias of that deity. She is also equated with {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s offspring deity {{ill|Uchi Prefecture Kami|ja|内県神|lt=Uchi Prefecture Kami}}. According to the 『Shuya Clan Genealogy』 (『守矢氏系譜』), established in the early Meiji period, Ōyatsuhime is the child of {{ill|Mote Takami|ja|洩宅神|lt=Mote Takami}} (son of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}) and is counted as the third generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}} who inherited ceremonial duties. Her name is said to originate from when her father hunted deer and captured 1,000 deer.
Mote Takami was born with extraordinary spiritual powers, took over his father's duties, carried bow and arrows, followed the Great Deity in hunting and game, and obtained a thousand deer. He had one son and named him Chikatō Kami. Chikatō Kami succeeded in overseeing ceremonial affairs. (Excerpt) Ancient [[Kagura|kagura]] songs: :Chikatō no Kitano Hayashinowa Susumushiwa Susumushiwa Yachiyo no Koyete Tsunenitaisenu :Chikatō no Myōjin Shiyushūreshito Ohosoransu Ohosoransu Yukitataima no Hana no Kiyomeyo Chikatō's shrines are located in the Suwa District's Nakatsusa: Aruga, Uehara, Hanahara, Yokobuki, Kyudo in the Higashichikuma District's Kanda and Hayashi. Situated in {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}}, there exists a shrine dedicated to him. In ancient times, he was the deity of over thirty villages within the district. His consort is {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}}. According to oral tradition, there is a shrine of him in the same area. {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}}, the offspring of the Great Deity's child {{ill|Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto|ja|片倉辺命|lt=Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto}}, is considered the fourth generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}}. He married {{ill|Mitasama Hime-no-Kami|ja|美都多麻比売神|lt=Mitasama Hime-no-Kami}}, the child of {{ill|Moritatsu-no-Kami|ja|守達神|lt=Moritatsu-no-Kami}}, and gave birth to {{ill|Hachikushi|ja|八櫛神|lt=Hachikushi}}.Suwa Education Association. "『守矢氏系譜』" 『Suwa Historical Documents Collection, Volume 28』, 1938, pp. 31-72.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp. 333-336.Imai Nohiku. "O Suwa-sama Faith and Kami no Naga" 『Gods' Village: Ancient Suwa Stories』, Kokusho Publishing, 1976, pp. 117-118.
According to the 『Genealogy』, Chikatō Kami married {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}} and moved to {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}} (now {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}}, {{ill|Kanda, Matsumoto|ja|神田|lt=Kanda}}), which suggests that Chikatō Kami was either exiled from Suwa or left voluntarily. It is also believed that the succession of Chikatō Kami by {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s grandchild {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}} indicates that the {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|lt=Suwa Clan}} (descendants of the mythological {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}) took over the local worship duties of indigenous forces. There are also traditions where the foreign-born Chikatō Kami married local daughters not only in Matsumoto but also in other places. A famous tale narrates that "Chikato Kami" from Shinshu came to {{ill|Akagiyama Shrine|ja|赤城神社|lt=Akagiyama Shrine}} and married the sister of the {{ill|Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神|lt=Akagi Shrine}}.Imai Nohiku. "Chikato-sama" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.325.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.342.. == Faith == According to Imai Nohiku's research on the distribution of {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}}, Chikatō Kami shrines are found not only in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} (13 shrines) but also in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (8 shrines), {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} (20 shrines), {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}} (7 shrines), and {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} (15 shrines), spreading from Kanto to Tohoku. They have various representations such as "Chikatō," "Chikata," "Chikato," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," and "Chikata," with pronunciations including "Chikata," "Chikatsu," "Chikato (u)," etc.Imai Nohiku. "Chikatō Kami Approach" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.326.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.343.. The distribution of Chikatō Kami shrines is characterized by their apparent movement along mountainous regions (such as the [[Yatsugatake|八ヶ岳]], [[Haruna Mountain|榛名山]], [[Akagi Mountain|赤城山]], [[Mount Ontake|男体山]], [[Yamigomezaki|八溝山]]) and is believed to have spread through the eastward expansion of hunting clans (descendants of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢族]], the so-called [[Mote Yago|洩矢族]]). As deities of hunting, they are closely related to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}. According to Imai's research, in Yamanashi, Saitama, and Gunma, {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} shrines coincide with Chikatō Kami shrines.Yamato Iwao. "Shinano Ancient History Study" 『Shinano Ancient History Study』, Meicho Publishing, 1990, pp.201-202.Imai Nohiku. "Survey Collection of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.212-281.. In addition to hunting deities from Suwa (Shinshu), there are regions where Chikatō Kami is also revered as a deity of agriculture and fire prevention, similar to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}, with many shrines worshiping them as {{ill|Ishibō|ja|石棒|lt=Ishibō}} deities.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp.347-349.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.300-301.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.338-339.Tanaka Moto. "Primitive Agricultural Ritual Elements in Mote Yashima Ceremonial System" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, p.192.. Moreover, many shrines have different main deities depending on the region, even within {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. There are still many unexplored shrine precincts and regions, leaving numerous mysteries unresolved. === Main Shrines === ==== Koshin Region ==== * {{ill|Kanda Chikatō Shrine|ja|神田千鹿頭神社|lt=Kanda Chikatō Shrine}}, {{ill|Hayashi Chikatō Shrine|ja|林千鹿頭神社|lt=Hayashi Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}} - Hataiwa) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Suwa City, Nagano|ja|諏訪市|lt=Suwa City}}, Toyoda Aruga) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|lt=Chino City}}, Chino Uehara) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|lt=Chino City}}, Yoneda Hanahara) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Yokobuki, Suwa City|ja|横吹|lt=Yokobuki}}, Suwa City) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Higashiina District, Ichigata Town|ja|上伊那郡|lt=Higashiina District, Ichigata Town}}, Higashiina District, Ichigata Town) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近津神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Kofu City, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kofu City}}, Kiyatecho) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Kofu City, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kofu City}}, Takamachi) ==== Kanto Region ==== * {{ill|Oogo Shrine|ja|大胡神社|lt=Oogo Shrine}} (Former {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}}) ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市|lt=Maebashi City}}, Kawaharaha) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kasukawa Town|ja|粕川町|lt=Kasukawa Town}}) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Battoi Town|ja|笂井町|lt=Battoi Town}}) * {{ill|Oyato Shrine|ja|親都神社|lt=Oyato Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District, Nakanojō Town|ja|吾妻郡|lt=Agatsuma District, Nakanojō Town}}) * {{ill|Chihō Shrine (Kazo Central)|ja|千方神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kazo City|ja|加須市|lt=Kazo City}}) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Kamijo)|ja|千勝社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Kamiji) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Honmachi)|ja|千勝神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Honmachi) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Yoshiba)|ja|千勝神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Yoshiba) * {{ill|Chikagata Shrine|ja|千形神社}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|熊谷市|lt=Kumagaya City}} - Honmachi) * {{ill|Chigata Shrine|ja|智賀都神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya City|ja|宇都宮市|lt=Utsunomiya City}} - Tokujircho) * {{ill|Chihō Shrine|ja|智方神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oyama City|ja|小山市|lt=Oyama City}} - Takei) * {{ill|Chiha Shrine|ja|血方神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oyama City|ja|小山市|lt=Oyama City}} - Taman) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine (Kanda)|ja|近津神社}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Omiya City|ja|久喜市|lt=Omiya City}}) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Tsukubaji)|ja|千勝神社}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tsukuba City|ja|つくば市|lt=Tsukuba City}} - Tomizaki) ==== Tohoku Region ==== * {{ill|Tudokohachishin Jinja|ja|都々古別神社|lt=Tudokohachishin Jinja}} - {{ill|Suwa District, Tobamachi|ja|東白川郡|lt=Suwa District, Tobamachi}} - Tobamachi * {{ill|Tudokohachishin Shrine|ja|岩峰山石神社|lt=Iwanose Shrine}} * {{ill|Hisayose Shrine (Chihashin Shrine)|ja|久来石神社}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Iwashimizu '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' are two {{ill|Kami|ja|神道の神|lt=Shinto deities}} appearing in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythological]] [[deity (Shinto)|female deities]]. In one book of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}} (father) and {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}} (brother). The sister goddess Ōyatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|大屋都比売神|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|大屋津姫命|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, and the sister goddess Tsumatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|抓津姫神|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|抓津姫命|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami|ja|都麻都比売命|lt=Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami|ja|爪津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫命|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, among others. Together with {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, under the command of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}}, they scattered tree species across all the mountains nationwide and returned to {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|lt=Kii Province}} (now {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}). == Overview == [[File:Moriya Family Tree - 守矢氏系図.png|thumb|170px|{{Center|The first five generations of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢氏]] in 『守矢氏系譜』 (from Mote Yago to Hachikushi Kami)}} In the folk traditions of the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|lt=Suwa region}} ([[ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}), Ōyatsuhime is considered the offspring deity of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}, or an alias of that deity. She is also equated with {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s offspring deity {{ill|Uchi Prefecture Kami|ja|内県神|lt=Uchi Prefecture Kami}}. According to the 『Shuya Clan Genealogy』 (『守矢氏系譜』), established in the early Meiji period, Ōyatsuhime is the child of {{ill|Mote Takami|ja|洩宅神|lt=Mote Takami}} (son of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}) and is counted as the third generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}} who inherited ceremonial duties. Her name is said to originate from when her father hunted deer and captured 1,000 deer.
Mote Takami was born with extraordinary spiritual powers, took over his father's duties, carried bow and arrows, followed the Great Deity in hunting and game, and obtained a thousand deer. He had one son and named him Chikatō Kami. Chikatō Kami succeeded in overseeing ceremonial affairs. (Excerpt) Ancient [[Kagura|kagura]] songs: :Chikatō no Kitano Hayashinowa Susumushiwa Susumushiwa Yachiyo no Koyete Tsunenitaisenu :Chikatō no Myōjin Shiyushūreshito Ohosoransu Ohosoransu Yukitataima no Hana no Kiyomeyo Chikatō's shrines are located in the Suwa District's Nakatsusa: Aruga, Uehara, Hanahara, Yokobuki, Kyudo in the Higashichikuma District's Kanda and Hayashi. Situated in {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}}, there exists a shrine dedicated to him. In ancient times, he was the deity of over thirty villages within the district. His consort is {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}}. According to oral tradition, there is a shrine of him in the same area. {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}}, the offspring of the Great Deity's child {{ill|Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto|ja|片倉辺命|lt=Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto}}, is considered the fourth generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}}. He married {{ill|Mitasama Hime-no-Kami|ja|美都多麻比売神|lt=Mitasama Hime-no-Kami}}, the child of {{ill|Moritatsu-no-Kami|ja|守達神|lt=Moritatsu-no-Kami}}, and gave birth to {{ill|Hachikushi|ja|八櫛神|lt=Hachikushi}}.Suwa Education Association. "『守矢氏系譜』" 『Suwa Historical Documents Collection, Volume 28』, 1938, pp. 31-72.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp. 333-336.Imai Nohiku. "O Suwa-sama Faith and Kami no Naga" 『Gods' Village: Ancient Suwa Stories』, Kokusho Publishing, 1976, pp. 117-118.
According to the 『Genealogy』, Chikatō Kami married {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}} and moved to {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}} (now {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}}, {{ill|Kanda, Matsumoto|ja|神田|lt=Kanda}}), which suggests that Chikatō Kami was either exiled from Suwa or left voluntarily. It is also believed that the succession of Chikatō Kami by {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s grandchild {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}} indicates that the {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|lt=Suwa Clan}} (descendants of the mythological {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}) took over the local worship duties of indigenous forces. There are also traditions where the foreign-born Chikatō Kami married local daughters not only in Matsumoto but also in other places. A famous tale narrates that "Chikato Kami" from Shinshu came to {{ill|Akagiyama Shrine|ja|赤城神社|lt=Akagiyama Shrine}} and married the sister of the {{ill|Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神|lt=Akagi Shrine}}.Imai Nohiku. "Chikato-sama" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.325.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.342.. == Faith == According to Imai Nohiku's research on the distribution of {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}}, Chikatō Kami shrines are found not only in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} (13 shrines) but also in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (8 shrines), {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} (20 shrines), {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}} (7 shrines), and {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} (15 shrines), spreading from Kanto to Tohoku. They have various representations such as "Chikatō," "Chikata," "Chikato," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," and "Chikata," with pronunciations including "Chikata," "Chikatsu," "Chikato (u)," etc.Imai Nohiku. "Chikatō Kami Approach" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.326.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.343.. The distribution of Chikatō Kami shrines is characterized by their apparent movement along mountainous regions (such as the [[Yatsugatake|八ヶ岳]], [[Haruna Mountain|榛名山]], [[Akagi Mountain|赤城山]], [[Mount Ontake|男体山]], [[Yamigomezaki|八溝山]]) and is believed to have spread through the eastward expansion of hunting clans (descendants of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢族]], the so-called [[Mote Yago|洩矢族]]). As deities of hunting, they are closely related to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}. According to Imai's research, in Yamanashi, Saitama, and Gunma, {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} shrines coincide with Chikatō Kami shrines.Yamato Iwao. "Shinano Ancient History Study" 『Shinano Ancient History Study』, Meicho Publishing, 1990, pp.201-202.Imai Nohiku. "Survey Collection of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.212-281.. In addition to hunting deities from Suwa (Shinshu), there are regions where Chikatō Kami is also revered as a deity of agriculture and fire prevention, similar to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}, with many shrines worshiping them as {{ill|Ishibō|ja|石棒|lt=Ishibō}} deities.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp.347-349.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.300-301.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.338-339.Tanaka Moto. "Primitive Agricultural Ritual Elements in Mote Yashima Ceremonial System" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, p.192.. Moreover, many shrines have different main deities depending on the region, even within {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. There are still many unexplored shrine precincts and regions, leaving numerous mysteries unresolved. === Main Shrines === ==== Koshin Region ==== * {{ill|Kanda Chikatō Shrine|ja|神田千鹿頭神社|lt=Kanda Chikatō Shrine}}, {{ill|Hayashi Chikatō Shrine|ja|林千鹿頭神社|lt=Hayashi Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}} - Hataiwa) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Suwa City, Nagano|ja|諏訪市|lt=Suwa City}}, Toyoda Aruga) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|lt=Chino City}}, Chino Uehara) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|lt=Chino City}}, Yoneda Hanahara) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Yokobuki, Suwa City|ja|横吹|lt=Yokobuki}}, Suwa City) * {{ill|Chikatō Shrine|ja|千鹿頭神社|lt=Chikatō Shrine}} ({{ill|Higashiina District, Ichigata Town|ja|上伊那郡|lt=Higashiina District, Ichigata Town}}, Higashiina District, Ichigata Town) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近津神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Kofu City, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kofu City}}, Kiyatecho) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Kofu City, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kofu City}}, Takamachi) ==== Kanto Region ==== * {{ill|Oogo Shrine|ja|大胡神社|lt=Oogo Shrine}} (Former {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}}) ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市|lt=Maebashi City}}, Kawaharaha) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kasukawa Town|ja|粕川町|lt=Kasukawa Town}}) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine|ja|近戸神社|lt=Chikata Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Battoi Town|ja|笂井町|lt=Battoi Town}}) * {{ill|Oyato Shrine|ja|親都神社|lt=Oyato Shrine}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District, Nakanojō Town|ja|吾妻郡|lt=Agatsuma District, Nakanojō Town}}) * {{ill|Chihō Shrine (Kazo Central)|ja|千方神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kazo City|ja|加須市|lt=Kazo City}}) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Kamijo)|ja|千勝社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Kamiji) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Honmachi)|ja|千勝神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Honmachi) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Kuki City's Yoshiba)|ja|千勝神社}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kuki City|ja|久喜市|lt=Kuki City}} - Yoshiba) * {{ill|Chikagata Shrine|ja|千形神社}} ({{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|熊谷市|lt=Kumagaya City}} - Honmachi) * {{ill|Chigata Shrine|ja|智賀都神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya City|ja|宇都宮市|lt=Utsunomiya City}} - Tokujircho) * {{ill|Chihō Shrine|ja|智方神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oyama City|ja|小山市|lt=Oyama City}} - Takei) * {{ill|Chiha Shrine|ja|血方神社}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oyama City|ja|小山市|lt=Oyama City}} - Taman) * {{ill|Chikata Shrine (Kanda)|ja|近津神社}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Omiya City|ja|久喜市|lt=Omiya City}}) * {{ill|Chishō Shrine (Tsukubaji)|ja|千勝神社}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tsukuba City|ja|つくば市|lt=Tsukuba City}} - Tomizaki) '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' are two {{ill|Kami|ja|神道の神|lt=Shinto deities}} appearing in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythological]] [[deity (Shinto)|female deities]]. In one book of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}} (father) and {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}} (brother). The sister goddess Ōyatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|大屋都比売神|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|大屋津姫命|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, and the sister goddess Tsumatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|抓津姫神|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|抓津姫命|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami|ja|都麻都比売命|lt=Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami|ja|爪津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫命|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, among others. Together with {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, under the command of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}}, they scattered tree species across all the mountains nationwide and returned to {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|lt=Kii Province}} (now {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}). == Overview == [[File:Moriya Family Tree - 守矢氏系図.png|thumb|170px|{{Center|The first five generations of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢氏]] in 『守矢氏系譜』 (from Mote Yago to Hachikushi Kami)}} In the folk traditions of the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|lt=Suwa region}} ([[ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}), Ōyatsuhime is considered the offspring deity of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}, or an alias of that deity. She is also equated with {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s offspring deity {{ill|Uchi Prefecture Kami|ja|内県神|lt=Uchi Prefecture Kami}}. According to the 『Shuya Clan Genealogy』 (『守矢氏系譜』), established in the early Meiji period, Ōyatsuhime is the child of {{ill|Mote Takami|ja|洩宅神|lt=Mote Takami}} (son of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}) and is counted as the third generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}} who inherited ceremonial duties. Her name is said to originate from when her father hunted deer and captured 1,000 deer.
Mote Takami was born with extraordinary spiritual powers, took over his father's duties, carried bow and arrows, followed the Great Deity in hunting and game, and obtained a thousand deer. He had one son and named him Chikatō Kami. Chikatō Kami succeeded in overseeing ceremonial affairs. (Excerpt) Ancient [[Kagura|kagura]] songs: :Chikatō no Kitano Hayashinowa Susumushiwa Susumushiwa Yachiyo no Koyete Tsunenitaisenu :Chikatō no Myōjin Shiyushūreshito Ohosoransu Ohosoransu Yukitataima no Hana no Kiyomeyo Chikatō's shrines are located in the Suwa District's Nakatsusa: Aruga, Uehara, Hanahara, Yokobuki, Kyudo in the Higashichikuma District's Kanda and Hayashi. Situated in {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}}, there exists a shrine dedicated to him. In ancient times, he was the deity of over thirty villages within the district. His consort is {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}}. According to oral tradition, there is a shrine of him in the same area. {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}}, the offspring of the Great Deity's child {{ill|Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto|ja|片倉辺命|lt=Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto}}, is considered the fourth generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}}. He married {{ill|Mitasama Hime-no-Kami|ja|美都多麻比売神|lt=Mitasama Hime-no-Kami}}, the child of {{ill|Moritatsu-no-Kami|ja|守達神|lt=Moritatsu-no-Kami}}, and gave birth to {{ill|Hachikushi|ja|八櫛神|lt=Hachikushi}}.Suwa Education Association. "『守矢氏系譜』" 『Suwa Historical Documents Collection, Volume 28』, 1938, pp. 31-72.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp. 333-336.Imai Nohiku. "O Suwa-sama Faith and Kami no Naga" 『Gods' Village: Ancient Suwa Stories』, Kokusho Publishing, 1976, pp. 117-118.
According to the 『Genealogy』, Chikatō Kami married {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}} and moved to {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}} (now {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}}, {{ill|Kanda, Matsumoto|ja|神田|lt=Kanda}}), which suggests that Chikatō Kami was either exiled from Suwa or left voluntarily. It is also believed that the succession of Chikatō Kami by {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s grandchild {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}} indicates that the {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|lt=Suwa Clan}} (descendants of the mythological {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}) took over the local worship duties of indigenous forces. There are also traditions where the foreign-born Chikatō Kami married local daughters not only in Matsumoto but also in other places. A famous tale narrates that "Chikato Kami" from Shinshu came to {{ill|Akagiyama Shrine|ja|赤城神社|lt=Akagiyama Shrine}} and married the sister of the {{ill|Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神|lt=Akagi Shrine}}.Imai Nohiku. "Chikato-sama" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.325.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.342.. == Faith == According to Imai Nohiku's research on the distribution of {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}}, Chikatō Kami shrines are found not only in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} (13 shrines) but also in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (8 shrines), {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} (20 shrines), {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}} (12 shrines), {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}} (7 shrines), and {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} (15 shrines), spreading from Kanto to Tohoku. They have various representations such as "Chikatō," "Chikata," "Chikato," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," "Chikata," and "Chikata," with pronunciations including "Chikata," "Chikatsu," "Chikato (u)," etc.Imai Nohiku. "Chikatō Kami Approach" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.326.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, p.343.. The distribution of Chikatō Kami shrines is characterized by their apparent movement along mountainous regions (such as the [[Yatsugatake|八ヶ岳]], [[Haruna Mountain|榛名山]], [[Akagi Mountain|赤城山]], [[Mount Ontake|男体山]], [[Yamigomezaki|八溝山]]) and is believed to have spread through the eastward expansion of hunting clans (descendants of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢族]], the so-called [[Mote Yago|洩矢族]]). As deities of hunting, they are closely related to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}. According to Imai's research, in Yamanashi, Saitama, and Gunma, {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} shrines coincide with Chikatō Kami shrines.Yamato Iwao. "Survey Collection of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.212-281.. In addition to hunting deities from Suwa (Shinshu), there are regions where Chikatō Kami is also revered as a deity of agriculture and fire prevention, similar to {{ill|Mishagujishin|ja|ミシャグジ信仰|lt=Mishagujishin}} and {{ill|Tenpaku Faith|ja|天白信仰|lt=Tenpaku Faith}}, with many shrines worshiping them as {{ill|Ishibō|ja|石棒|lt=Ishibō}} deities.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp.347-349.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.300-301.Imai Nohiku. "Survey of Shrine Priests" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, pp.338-339.Tanaka Moto. "Primitive Agricultural Ritual Elements in Mote Yashima Ceremonial System" 『Study on Ancient Suwa and Mishagujis' Ceremonial Systems』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2017, p.192.. Moreover, many shrines have different main deities depending on the region, even within {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' are two {{ill|Kami|ja|神道の神|lt=Shinto deities}} appearing in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythological]] [[deity (Shinto)|female deities]]. In one book of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}} (father) and {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}} (brother). The sister goddess Ōyatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|大屋都比売神|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|大屋津姫命|lt=Ōyatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, and the sister goddess Tsumatsuhime is referred to as {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|抓津姫神|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|抓津姫命|lt=Tsumatsuhime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami|ja|都麻都比売命|lt=Tsumamatu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami|ja|爪津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫神|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|枛津姫命|lt=Tsumatsu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, among others. Together with {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isonotakeru no mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}'''|五十猛命|}}, under the command of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Susanoo}}, they scattered tree species across all the mountains nationwide and returned to {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|lt=Kii Province}} (now {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}). == Overview == [[File:Moriya Family Tree - 守矢氏系図.png|thumb|170px|{{Center|The first five generations of the [[Moriya Clan|守矢氏]] in 『守矢氏系譜』 (from Mote Yago to Hachikushi Kami)}}]] In the folk traditions of the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|lt=Suwa region}} ([[ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}), Ōyatsuhime is considered the offspring deity of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}, or an alias of that deity. She is also equated with {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s offspring deity {{ill|Uchi Prefecture Kami|ja|内県神|lt=Uchi Prefecture Kami}}. According to the 『Shuya Clan Genealogy』 (『守矢氏系譜』), established in the early Meiji period, Ōyatsuhime is the child of {{ill|Mote Takami|ja|洩宅神|lt=Mote Takami}} (son of {{ill|Mote Yago|ja|洩矢神|lt=Mote Yago}}) and is counted as the third generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}} who inherited ceremonial duties. Her name is said to originate from when her father hunted deer and captured 1,000 deer.
Mote Takami was born with extraordinary spiritual powers, took over his father's duties, carried bow and arrows, followed the Great Deity in hunting and game, and obtained a thousand deer. He had one son and named him Chikatō Kami. Chikatō Kami succeeded in overseeing ceremonial affairs. (Excerpt) Ancient [[Kagura|kagura]] songs: :Chikatō no Kitano Hayashinowa Susumushiwa Susumushiwa Yachiyo no Koyete Tsunenitaisenu :Chikatō no Myōjin Shiyushūreshito Ohosoransu Ohosoransu Yukitataima no Hana no Kiyomeyo Chikatō's shrines are located in the Suwa District's Nakatsusa: Aruga, Uehara, Hanahara, Yokobuki, Kyudo in the Higashichikuma District's Kanda and Hayashi. Situated in {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}}, there exists a shrine dedicated to him. In ancient times, he was the deity of over thirty villages within the district. His consort is {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}}. According to oral tradition, there is a shrine of him in the same area. {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}}, the offspring of the Great Deity's child {{ill|Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto|ja|片倉辺命|lt=Katakura Hade-no-Mikoto}}, is considered the fourth generation of the {{ill|Moriya Clan|ja|守矢氏|lt=Moriya Clan}}. He married {{ill|Mitasama Hime-no-Kami|ja|美都多麻比売神|lt=Mitasama Hime-no-Kami}}, the child of {{ill|Moritatsu-no-Kami|ja|守達神|lt=Moritatsu-no-Kami}}, and gave birth to {{ill|Hachikushi|ja|八櫛神|lt=Hachikushi}}.Suwa Education Association. "『守矢氏系譜』" 『Suwa Historical Documents Collection, Volume 28』, 1938, pp. 31-72.Nomoto Mikato. "Approach to Chikatō Kami" 『Study on the Emergence and Development of Suwa Faith』, Ancient Tribe Research Society edited by Jinkan-sha, 2018, pp. 333-336.Imai Nohiku. "O Suwa-sama Faith and Kami no Naga" 『Gods' Village: Ancient Suwa Stories』, Kokusho Publishing, 1976, pp. 117-118.
According to the 『Genealogy』, Chikatō Kami married {{ill|Uragokohime-no-Mikoto|ja|宇良古比売命|lt=Uragokohime-no-Mikoto}} and moved to {{ill|Urakoyama|ja|宇良古山|lt=Urakoyama}} (now {{ill|Matsumoto City|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto City}}, {{ill|Kanda, Matsumoto|ja|神田|lt=Kanda}}), which suggests that Chikatō Kami was either exiled from Suwa or left voluntarily. It is also believed that the succession of Chikatō Kami by {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}'s grandchild {{ill|Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto|ja|児玉彦命|lt=Kodama Hiko-no-Mikoto}} indicates that the {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|lt=Suwa Clan}} (descendants of the mythological {{ill|Take Minakata|ja|建御名方神|lt=Take Minakata}}) took over the local worship duties of indigenous forces. There are also traditions where the foreign-born Chikatō Kami married local daughters not only in Matsumoto but also in other places. A famous tale narrates that "Chikato Kami" from Shinshu came to {{ill|Akagiyama Shrine|ja|赤城神社|lt=Akagiyama Shrine}} and married the sister of the {{ill|Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神|lt=Akagi Shrine}}.Imai Nohiku. '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.

'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:Kinto Maki]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Kinto Maki]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]] [[Category:Oldest 50 pages as of February 16 2025]] [[Category:qq--qq]]

revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]] [[Category:Oldest 50 pages as of February 16 2025]] [[Category:qq--qq]]
