== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:11]] [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]] [[Category:Oldest 50 pages as of February 16 2025]]

revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:11]] [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]] [[Category:Oldest 50 pages as of February 16 2025]]

revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:11]] [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Return the see also]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=draft}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=draft}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市|12=draft}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|12=draft}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=draft}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=draft}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=draft}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|12=draft}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] }} [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=draft|12=draft}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] }} [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|12=draft" to "|qq=draft"
revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] }} [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

revision.comment: 27 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ up to rev 84129424
revision.text:
'''オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ'''は、[[日本神話]]に登場する樹木の[[神 (神道)|女神]]。『[[日本書紀]]』の一書では父神・[[スサノオ|素戔嗚尊]]、兄神・[[五十猛神|五十猛命]]と記されている。姉神のオオヤツヒメは大屋都比賣神、大屋津姫命、妹神のツマツヒメは抓津姫神、抓津姫命、都麻都比賣命、爪津姫神、枛津姫神、枛津姫命などと表記する。 五十猛命と共に素戔嗚尊の命により全国の山々に木種を撒き、[[紀伊国]]（現在の[[和歌山県]]）に戻って住んだとされる。 == 概要 == オオヤツヒメの神名の「ヤ」とは、家屋の事であり、彼女が樹木を司る神であると同時に、樹木から作られる建造物、家屋や船などの神である事を示している。ツマツヒメの「ツマ」は建物を築くために製材した材木を意味する。 また、木種を全国に撒く事は、子供を産む力を持った女性として、彼女たちは生殖を司る神でもある事を意味する。現在においては、兄神五十猛命と共に木の文化を司るとされ、[[林業]]や[[建設業|建築業]]の女神として信仰を集めている。 [[島根県]][[大田市]]大屋町の地名はオオヤツヒメの名に因むものとされる。 == 神話における記述 == 『日本書紀』 卷第一[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm 日本書紀 卷第一 神代一] 第八段の第五の一書では、以下のとおり。
一書曰 素戔嗚尊曰 韓郷之嶋 是有金銀 若使吾兒所御之國 不有浮寶者 未是佳也 乃拔鬚髯散之 即成杉 又拔散胸毛 是成檜 尻毛是成柀 眉毛是成櫲樟 已而定其當用 乃稱之曰 杉及櫲樟 此兩樹者 可以爲浮寶 檜可以爲瑞宮之材 柀可以爲顯見蒼生奥津棄戸將臥之具 夫須噉八十木種 皆能播生 于時 素戔嗚尊之子 號曰五十猛命 妹大屋津姫命 次枛津姫命 凡此三神 亦能分布木種 即奉渡於紀伊國也 然後 素戔嗚尊 居熊成峯 而遂入於根國者矣棄戸 此云須多杯 柀 此云磨紀
[[日本紀講筵]]の際提出された[[偽書]]とされる『[[先代旧事本紀]]』巻第四 地祇本紀[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm 先代舊事本紀卷第四 地祇本紀]の記述は以下のとおり。
一説曰 素戔烏尊之子 號曰 五十猛命 妹 大屋姫命 次 抓津姫命 凡三神 亦能分布八十木種 則奉渡於紀伊國 及此國所祭之神是也
素戔烏尊 此尊與天照太神共誓約（中略）次 五十猛命 亦云 大屋彦神 次 大屋姫神 次 抓津姫神 已上三柱 並坐 紀伊國 則紀伊國造齋祠神也
==祀られている神社== * [[大屋都姫神社]] * [[都麻都比売神社|都麻津姫神社]] * [[伊太祁曽神社|伊太祁󠄀曽神社]]（摂社扱い） * [[売布神社 (松江市)|売布神社]]（相殿） * [[妻山神社]]（ツマツヒメのみ） == 脚注 == ==関連項目== *[[日本の神の一覧]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやつひめつまつひめ}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:草木の神]] [[Category:女神]]

[[日本紀講筵]]の際提出された[[偽書]]とされる『[[先代旧事本紀]]』巻第四 地祇本紀[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm 先代舊事本紀卷第四 地祇本紀]の記述は以下のとおり。
一説曰 素戔烏尊之子 號曰 五十猛命 妹 大屋姫命 次 抓津姫命 凡三神 亦能分布八十木種 則奉渡於紀伊國 及此國所祭之神是也
素戔烏尊 此尊與天照太神共誓約（中略）次 五十猛命 亦云 大屋彦神 次 大屋姫神 次 抓津姫神 已上三柱 並坐 紀伊國 則紀伊國造齋祠神也
==祀られている神社== * [[大屋都姫神社]] * [[都麻都比売神社|都麻津姫神社]] * [[伊太祁曽神社|伊太祁󠄀曽神社]]（摂社扱い） * [[売布神社 (松江市)|売布神社]]（相殿） * [[妻山神社]]（ツマツヒメのみ） == 脚注 == ==関連項目== *[[日本の神の一覧]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおやつひめつまつひめ}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:草木の神]] [[Category:女神]]

revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] }} [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}}

He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] }} [[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] }} [[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]]

Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.

revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|qq=}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefecture|qq=}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|ja|大田市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōda, Shimane|qq=}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|qq=}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Kuji Hongi|qq=}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.

revision.comment: Bot: ill fix final-12
revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|qq=}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Deities of plants and trees]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]] [[Category:草木の神]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove obsolete category Qq
revision.text:
'''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|qq=}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[ja:%E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]] [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "paes with no wikidata" to "Pages with no Wikidata"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|qq=}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[sn:E3%82%AA%E3%82%AA%E3%83%A4%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BB%E3%83%84%E3%83%9E%E3%83%84%E3%83%92%E3%83%A1]] [[ja:大屋都姫・抓津姫]] [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[sn:Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]]

In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.

He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:

If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:

Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|qq=|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|qq=|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}}

One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.

revision.text:
[[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}}

== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]
