== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
[[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Goddesses]] }} [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
[[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in [[:en:Ōda, Shimane|Ōda, Shimane]] is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")

One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|en|Ōda, Shimane|WD=Q384796|lt=Ōda, Shimane}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.

revision.text:
[[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]][[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] '''Ōyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime''' (大屋都姫・抓津姫) are [[Japanese mythology|Shinto goddesses]] of trees. According to one account in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, they are recorded as the daughters of {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and younger sisters of {{ill|Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja|五十猛命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitake-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五十猛神|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=五十猛神|4=zh|5=五十猛神}}. The elder sister, Ōyatsuhime, is also written as 大屋都比賣神 or 大屋津姫命, while the younger sister, Tsumatsuhime, appears as 抓津姫神, 抓津姫命, 都麻都比賣命, 爪津姫神, 枛津姫神, or 枛津姫命. Together with Iitake-no-Mikoto, they are said to have scattered seeds of trees across the mountains of Japan under the command of Susanoo and later resided in {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}). == Overview == The name "Ya" in Ōyatsuhime refers to buildings, signifying her role as a deity of trees and the structures made from them, such as houses and ships. The "Tsuma" in Tsumatsuhime represents the lumber processed for construction. Their act of scattering seeds also symbolizes their roles as goddesses of fertility and procreation. Today, they are venerated alongside their brother Iitake-no-Mikoto as deities of forestry and construction industries. The place name Ōya-chō in {{ill|Ōda, Shimane|en|Ōda, Shimane|WD=Q384796|lt=Ōda, Shimane|1=Ōda, Shimane}} is said to derive from Ōyatsuhime. == Mythological Accounts == In {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} Volume I[http://home.p07.itscom.net/strmdrf/nihonsyoki.htm Nihon Shoki Volume I], the eighth section of the fifth account states as follows:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto said, “In the island of {{ill|Kara|ja|韓郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, there are gold and silver. If the country where my children will rule does not have such floating treasures, it is not good.” He then pulled out his beard and scattered it, creating cedar trees. He pulled out his chest hair and scattered it, creating cypresses. His back hair became hinoki, and his eyebrow hair became camphor trees. He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
== Shrines Dedicated to the Deities == * {{ill|Ōyatsuhime Shrine|ja|大屋都姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyatsuhime Shrine|1=Oyatsuhime Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434556}} * {{ill|Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja|都麻津姫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumatsuhime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso shrine|lt=Itakiso Shrine|ja|伊太祁󠄀曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakiso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (as an auxiliary shrine) * {{ill|Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|ja|売布神社 (松江市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meifu Shrine (Matsue)|1=Mefu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429659}} (shared enshrinement) * {{ill|Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja|妻山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Mountain Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tsumatsuhime only) == Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ|version=84129424|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:オオヤツヒメ・ツマツヒメ]] [[zh:大屋津姬命與枛津姬命]] {{wikidata link|Q10935047}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Vegetation gods]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

He then established their uses, declaring, “Cedar and camphor trees can serve as floating treasures. Hinoki can be used to build sacred palaces. Maki trees can provide materials for the humble abodes of the people.” Susanoo’s children—known as Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime, and Tsumatsuhime—also scattered seeds across the land and offered them to Kii Province. Later, Susanoo resided at Kumano’s peaks and descended into the underworld. (The term "Kito" is pronounced "Sudahai." "Maki" is pronounced "Makibi.")
In the pseudo-historical text {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}} Volume IV[http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/sendaikuji04.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Volume IV], the following is stated:
One account says: Susanoo-no-Mikoto’s children were Iitake-no-Mikoto, Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime. These three deities scattered the seeds of eighty types of trees and offered them to Kii Province. The deities enshrined in this province are these three.
Susanoo-no-Mikoto, along with Amaterasu-Ōmikami, swore an oath... The three deities, Iitake-no-Mikoto (also called Ōyahiko), Ōyahime, and Tsumatsuhime, are enshrined in Kii Province as the deities of its local shrines.
